engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes-AMG GT C190 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: The M178 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 — in every C190 variant from the entry-level GT to the track-focused GT R and the ultimate Black Series.

engines.variants: GT, GT S, GT C, GT R, GT R Pro, GT Black Series

overview.intro: The Mercedes-AMG GT C190 is the two-door coupé variant of AMG's second in-house sports car, succeeding the SLS AMG with a front-mid-engine layout and a transaxle seven-speed dual-clutch transmission. Unveiled in October 2014 at the Paris Motor Show, the C190 launched with the M178 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 — AMG's first ground-up engine design — and evolved across six distinct variant levels from the entry-level GT through to the track-focused Black Series. All C190s share the same 3,982cc V8 block, with power outputs ranging from 462 bhp to 730 bhp depending on factory tune, turbocharger hardware and engine-management calibration.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-AMG C190 (front-mid-engine, transaxle) • Predecessor: SLS AMG • Successor: C192 (second-generation AMG GT coupé) • Sibling body styles in this generation: R190 Roadster.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The C190 coupé attracts strong specialist interest in the UK used market, with M178 V8 enquiries concentrated on pre-purchase inspections and high-mileage maintenance [PATTERN DATA].

items.quote: Thin-Data Fallback mode applied — compressed to a single paragraph. Best & Worst Engines: The M178.980 is the only engine fitted to the C190, so the choice is not which engine but which variant. The GT (462 bhp) offers the lowest entry cost and the gentlest tune, making it the most sensible daily driver. The GT R (585 bhp) and GT R Pro represent the sweet spot for track-focused buyers — more durable than the 730 bhp Black Series and less stressed than its extreme stablemate. The Black Series, while spectacular, operates at the very limit of the M178's design envelope — a £15,000+ engine replacement is a genuine possibility for owners who track them hard. For most buyers, the GT S (510 bhp) or GT C (550 bhp) offers the best balance of performance and mechanical sympathy. Who it's for: buyers who want AMG V8 theatre without the Black Series's running-cost anxiety.

economicsRule.title: The C190 rule
economicsRule.text: A £10,000 M178 recon against a £45,000 C190 GT sits at 22% of the car's value — well within Mercedes-Benz's 70% threshold. Against a £70,000 GT R, the same recon drops to 14%. Against a £30,000 high-mileage GT, it reaches 33% — still safe. The C190's strong retained values mean engine replacement is almost always financially viable. The real question is whether you're buying a GT or a Black Series: the entry-level car's replacement cost is a manageable percentage; the top-end car's running costs are a different conversation entirely.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C190 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: GT (462 bhp) — lowest tune, lowest stress, cheapest parts.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C190 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: Black Series — extreme tune, extreme running costs, extreme depreciation on high-mileage cars.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C190 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: GT C (550 bhp) — the sweet spot between performance and running costs.

problems.title: Carbon-Fibre Propshaft Bonding Failure
problems.affectedModels: All C190 variants (GT, GT S, GT C, GT R, Black Series) — 2014–2022 production.
problems.rootCause: The carbon-fibre propshaft is bonded to the engine/transmission flange. Silicone residue on the bonding surfaces can impair adhesive strength, leading to separation between the propshaft and the flange during driving — resulting in a complete loss of drive. Mercedes-AMG issued a recall campaign addressing this manufacturing defect.
problems.recommendation: Check the recall status of any C190 before purchase — if the propshaft bonding recall hasn't been performed, treat it as immediate preventative maintenance. A £1,500–£2,500 inspection and replacement is cheaper than the £3,000–£5,000 repair that follows a failure on the road.

problems.title: M178 V8 Oil Leaks (Valve Cover & Turbo Oil Lines)
problems.affectedModels: Higher-mileage C190s, particularly GT/GT S variants over 40,000 miles.
problems.rootCause: The M178's hot-vee design concentrates heat between the cylinder banks, accelerating gasket and seal degradation on valve covers and turbocharger oil feed lines.
problems.recommendation: Have valve covers and turbo oil lines inspected at every service — a £800–£1,500 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 top-end reseal that follows oil starvation.

problems.title: AMG SPEEDSHIFT DCT Transmission Hesitation
problems.affectedModels: Early C190s (2015–2017), particularly GT and GT S.
problems.rootCause: The seven-speed dual-clutch transmission can develop hesitation or harsh downshifts under load, particularly when hot. Software calibration and, in some cases, clutch wear are the primary contributors.
problems.recommendation: If your early C190 exhibits hesitation, start with a software update and adaptation reset — £300–£700 is cheap insurance against a £5,500+ transmission replacement.

replacementCosts.h2: C190 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

items.question: How much does a Mercedes-AMG GT C190 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned M178.980 engine for the C190 typically costs between £8,000 and £18,000 supply only [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet], depending on the variant (GT to Black Series). Fitting adds approximately 14–22 hours of labour at specialist rates — budget an additional £1,000–£1,500 for installation. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

items.question: Which Mercedes-AMG GT C190 engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: All C190s share the same M178.980 block, so reliability depends on the factory tune. The entry-level GT (462 bhp) is the least stressed and most durable in the long term. The Black Series (730 bhp) pushes the engine to its absolute limit — impressive, but it carries a higher risk of premature wear if tracked hard.

items.question: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-AMG GT C190?
items.answer: The most significant C190‑specific issue is the carbon‑fibre propshaft bonding failure, which can cause a total loss of drive — a recall campaign addressed this. Other common faults include oil leaks from valve covers and turbo lines (hot‑vee heat degradation), and early DCT transmission hesitation that often resolves with a software update.

items.question: Is the Mercedes-AMG GT C190 reliable?
items.answer: Yes, for a high‑performance AMG V8. The M178 is a robust engine when maintained properly. Its Achilles’ heel is the propshaft bonding (now mostly rectified via recall) and heat‑related seal degradation — both manageable with preventative checks. With careful ownership, a C190 can be enjoyed without catastrophic failures.

items.question: Which Mercedes-AMG GT C190 variant should I avoid?
items.answer: No variant is inherently “bad”, but the Black Series is the most demanding — its 730 bhp tune makes it expensive to maintain and any engine issue costs significantly more. For everyday use, the GT or GT S offer the best balance; for track focus, the GT R is a safer bet than the Black Series on running costs.

items.question: How do I know if my C190 engine needs replacing?
items.answer: Key warning signs include loss of drive (propshaft bonding failure), persistent oil leaks under the car, blue smoke from the exhaust, or a check engine light with misfires. A sudden drop in power or unusual knocking noises from the V8 also warrant immediate specialist inspection — use our diagnostic tool for an initial assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a C190 engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Given the C190’s strong retained values, replacement is almost always financially viable. A £10,000 recon on a £45,000 GT is just 22% of the car’s value — well below our 70% Mercedes‑Benz threshold. Even on a high‑mileage example, the cost rarely exceeds the car’s worth. The decision becomes subjective if the car has other significant issues.

items.question: How long do Mercedes-AMG GT C190 engines last?
items.answer: With regular servicing and sensible use, the M178 can comfortably exceed 100,000 miles. Many owners report trouble‑free running beyond 60,000 miles. The limiting factors are the propshaft bonding (recall‑fixed on most cars) and oil leaks — both preventable with proactive maintenance. Track‑heavy use will shorten that lifespan.

items.question: What is the difference between the C190 and R190?
items.answer: Both are first‑generation AMG GT models sharing the M178 engine and platform. The C190 is the fixed‑roof coupé (2014–2022), while the R190 is the open‑top roadster (2015–2023). Labour access and some component costs differ slightly due to body‑style reinforcements — see the R190 generation page for its specific data.

items.question: How do I find my Mercedes-AMG GT C190 engine code?
items.answer: The M178.980 engine code is stamped on the engine block (visible from underneath, near the oil pan) and listed on the vehicle’s VIN plate and registration documents. Enter your variant and year into our engine‑finder tool, and we’ll confirm which tune you have — our network of 25+ vetted AMG specialists can verify it for free.
